content:
# Romans Chapter 8 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_008_page_001.jpg

1. There is therefore now no condemnation to the [ones] in Christos Yeshua.

2. For the law of the Spirit of the life in Christos Yeshua freed you from the law of the sin and of the death.

3. For the powerlessness of the law, in that it was weak through the flesh — the God, having sent the Son of himself in [the] likeness of sinful flesh and concerning sin, condemned the sin in the flesh,

4. that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us, the [ones] not walking according to [the] flesh but according to [the] Spirit.

5. For the [ones] being according to [the] flesh mind the things of the flesh; but the [ones] according to [the] Spirit, the things of the Spirit.

6. For the mind of the flesh [is] death, but the mind of the Spirit [is] life and peace.

7. Because the mind of the flesh [is] enmity toward God; for it does not submit to the law of the God, for neither is it able [to].

8. And the [ones] being in [the] flesh are not able to please God.

9. But you are not in [the] flesh but in [the] Spirit, if indeed [the] Spirit of God dwells in you. But if anyone does not have [the] Spirit of Christos, this [one] is not of him.

10. But if Christos [is] in you, the body indeed [is] dead because of sin, but the Spirit [is] life because of righteousness.

11. And if the Spirit of the [one] having raised Yeshua from [the] dead dwells in you, the [one] having raised Christos from [the] dead will also make alive the mortal bodies of you through the Spirit of him dwelling in you.

12. So then, brothers, we are debtors — not to the flesh, to live according to [the] flesh.

13. For if you live according to [the] flesh, you are about to die; but if by [the] Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.

14. For as many as are led by [the] Spirit of God, these are sons of God.

15. For you did not receive a spirit of slavery again to fear, but you received a Spirit of adoption, in which we cry, "Abba, the Father!"

16. The Spirit himself bears witness together with the spirit of us that we are children of God.

17. And if children, also heirs — heirs indeed of God, and co-heirs with Christos, if indeed we suffer together [with him], that we may also be glorified together [with him].

18. For I reckon that the sufferings of the present time [are] not worthy [of comparison] to the glory about to be revealed to us.

19. For the eager expectation of the creation awaits the revelation of the sons of the God.

20. For the creation was subjected to futility — not willingly, but because of the [one] having subjected [it] — upon hope,

21. because the creation itself also will be freed from the slavery of the corruption into the freedom of the glory of the children of the God.

22. For we know that all the creation groans together and suffers birth-pangs together until the present.

23. And not only [this], but also we ourselves, having the firstfruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves groan within ourselves, awaiting [the] adoption — the redemption of the body of us.

24. For by the hope we were saved; but hope being seen is not hope; for what someone sees, why does he also hope [for it]?

25. But if what we do not see we hope [for], through endurance we await [it].

26. And likewise also the Spirit helps the weakness of us. For what we should pray as it is necessary, we do not know; but the Spirit himself intercedes with groanings unutterable.

27. And the [one] searching the hearts knows what [is] the mind of the Spirit, because according to God he intercedes for [the] holy [ones].

28. And we know that to the [ones] loving the God, all things work together for good, to the [ones] being called according to [his] purpose.

29. Because whom he foreknew, he also predestined [to be] conformed to the image of the Son of him, so that he [would] be [the] firstborn among many brothers.

30. And whom he predestined, these he also called; and whom he called, these he also justified; and whom he justified, these he also glorified.

31. What then shall we say to these things? If the God [is] for us, who [is] against us?

32. He who indeed did not spare the Son of himself, but for us all handed him over — how will he not also with him freely give us all things?

33. Who will bring a charge against [the] chosen [ones] of God? [It is] God, the [one] justifying.

34. Who [is] the [one] condemning? [It is] Christos Yeshua, the [one] having died — and more, having been raised — who is at [the] right [hand] of the God, who also intercedes for us.

35. Who will separate us from the love of the Christos? Tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword?

36. Just as it has been written, "For your sake we are being put to death the whole day; we were reckoned as sheep of slaughter."

37. But in all these things we overwhelmingly conquer through the [one] having loved us.

38. For I am persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things coming, nor powers,

39. nor height nor depth, nor any other created thing, will be able to separate us from the love of the God, the [one] in Christos Yeshua the Lord of us.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — Sinaiticus reading:** Sinaiticus omits the later addition "who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit" found in many manuscripts. The shorter reading (just "no condemnation to those in Christ Jesus") is original. The added phrase was imported from v.4.

2. **v.2 — "Freed you" (ἠλευθέρωσέν σε):** Sinaiticus reads the second person singular "you" (σε). Other manuscripts read "me" (με). The textual variation is minor but the Sinaiticus reading addresses the reader directly.

3. **v.15 — "Abba, the Father":** Αββα ὁ πατήρ. The Aramaic Abba preserved in Greek, followed by the Greek translation. This bilingual formula appears also in Mark 14:36 and Galatians 4:6. It reflects the earliest Christian prayer language — the Spirit enabling believers to address God with Jesus' own intimate term.

4. **v.28 — "All things work together for good":** πάντα συνεργεῖ εἰς ἀγαθόν. Some manuscripts read "God works all things together for good" (ὁ θεὸς συνεργεῖ). The Sinaiticus reading has "all things" as the subject.

5. **v.35 — "Love of Christ" (ἀγάπης τοῦ Χριστοῦ):** Objective or subjective genitive? "Our love for Christ" or "Christ's love for us"? Context favors the latter — it is Christ's love from which nothing can separate us.

6. **vv.38-39 — The catalogue:** Paul lists ten possible separators — none succeeds. The catalogue moves from human experience (death, life) to cosmic powers (angels, rulers, powers) to temporal categories (present, future) to spatial dimensions (height, depth). Nothing in all creation can break the bond.

— EMET, AI Translator
